guest_credentials: Cycling coach at World Tour team Bora-Hansgrohe, formerly at Science to Sport and UAE Team Emirates, works with elite riders including Primož Roglič and Jay Hindley
key_claims: Training for ultra-endurance events should prioritize neuromuscular efforts and metabolic conditioning on the bike, with significant off-bike strength work throughout the training block
Cadence decreases as fatigue accumulates in ultra events; athletes must build torque-based power through strength training to maintain forward momentum when high cadence becomes impossible

The submaximal fatigue test (3-minute effort at threshold-to-110% threshold power, conducted every 7-10 days) tracks whether training stimulus is correct, the athlete is fatigued, or progression has stagnated

Professional cyclists distinguish themselves through attention to detail and structured professionalism; 'how you do anything is how you do everything' applies from bike cleanliness to tire pressure decisions
Amateur cyclists should focus on sustainable power in a position they can hold for the entire duration rather than adopting aggressive aerodynamic positions they cannot maintain
specific_numbers: 6 hours per week training time constraint example
Rob Britain average power winning Badlands: approximately 190 watts
Submaximal fatigue test: 3-minute duration at threshold to 110% threshold power
Periodization: 3+1 or 4+1 block structure (three or four weeks on, one week off)
One athlete completed 4,000 km nonstop event
24-hour mountain bike race as endurance example
cluster_reasoning: John Wakefield's discussion of periodization, submaximal fatigue testing, neuromuscular efforts, metabolic conditioning, and structured training methodology is core coaching content; strength work is secondary because torque-based power development and off-bike conditioning are integral to the endurance-building system he describes.
